subject: islamic-studies
description: Important Battles Patrols Invasions MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: On which date did the Battle of Badr take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Badr took place on 17th Ramadan, 2 A.H. (March 17, 624 AD). It was the first major battle of Islam and resulted in a decisive Muslim victory against the Quraysh of Makkah.

mcqs.q: How many Muslims participated in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: 313 Muslims participated in the Battle of Badr, facing an opposing force of 950 Quraysh fighters. Despite being outnumbered approximately 1:3, the Muslims won a decisive victory. This number 313 is one of the most frequently tested facts in competitive exams.

mcqs.q: How many Quraysh fighters opposed the Muslims in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: 950 Quraysh fighters faced the 313 Muslims in the Battle of Badr. The Muslims were outnumbered by approximately 3 to 1, yet they achieved a complete victory, which the Quran describes as divine assistance.

mcqs.q: How many Muslims were martyred in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: 14 Muslims were martyred in the Battle of Badr. In contrast, 70 Quraysh were killed and 70 were taken as prisoners. This asymmetric outcome was seen as a miraculous victory.

mcqs.q: Which prominent Companion did NOT take part in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Uthman bin Affan (RA) did not take part in the Battle of Badr. He stayed behind in Madinah to nurse his ill wife, Hazrat Ruqaiya (RA) — the Prophet's daughter. The Prophet (PBUH) counted him as a participant and assigned him a share of the spoils.

mcqs.q: Who was the oldest Companion in the front line at the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Ubaidah (RA) was the oldest in age and stood in the front line at the Battle of Badr. He was among the senior Companions known for their bravery and sacrifice in the early battles of Islam.

mcqs.q: Who was taken prisoner from the Muslim side in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Aqeel ibn Abi Talib (a brother of Hazrat Ali RA) was taken prisoner in the Battle of Badr by the Quraysh forces. He had not yet accepted Islam at that time. He later embraced Islam.

mcqs.q: Which daughter of the Holy Prophet died on the victory day of Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Ruqaiya (RA), the second daughter of the Holy Prophet (PBUH), died on the very day of the Battle of Badr victory (17th Ramadan, 2 A.H.). She had been ill and Hazrat Uthman (RA) stayed back to nurse her, which is why he missed the battle.

mcqs.q: Who finally killed Abu Jahl in the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Abu Jahl was fatally wounded by Mu'awwidh ibn Afra' and Mu'adh ibn Amr ibn al-Jamuh, and was then finished off by Abdullah ibn Masud (RA), who beheaded him. Abu Jahl was one of the fiercest enemies of early Islam.

mcqs.q: How many Quraysh were killed and captured respectively at the Battle of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: In the Battle of Badr, 70 Quraysh were killed and 70 were taken captive. This is a key number pair tested in competitive exams. The 70 prisoners' fate was debated: Abu Bakr (RA) recommended ransom, while Umer (RA) recommended execution.

mcqs.q: How many days did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) stay at Badr after the battle?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) stayed at Badr for 3 days after the victory. During this period, the martyrs were buried and the prisoners' matters were arranged.

mcqs.q: Who advised the Holy Prophet to take ransom and free the prisoners of Badr?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Abu Bakr (RA) advised the Holy Prophet (PBUH) to take ransom and release the prisoners of Badr. Hazrat Umer (RA) disagreed and wanted them executed. The Prophet agreed with Abu Bakr, though later Quranic verses indicated Umar's view was sterner.

mcqs.q: When did the Battle of Uhud take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Uhud took place on 3rd Shawwal, 3 A.H. (625 AD). It was fought on the slopes of Mount Uhud, about 5 kilometres north of Madinah.

mcqs.q: How many Muslims participated in the Battle of Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: 700 Muslims participated in the Battle of Uhud. The original force was 1000, but the hypocrite leader Abdullah ibn Ubay separated with 300 men, leaving only 700 to fight against 3,000 Quraysh soldiers.

mcqs.q: How many Muslims were martyred in the Battle of Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: 70 Muslims were martyred in the Battle of Uhud — exactly five times the number martyred at Badr (14). Among the most notable martyrs was Hazrat Hamzah (RA), the Prophet's uncle, known as Syed-ul-Shuhada.

mcqs.q: How many teeth did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) lose in the Battle of Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) lost 4 teeth in the Battle of Uhud. He was also struck on the face, causing him to bleed. Despite his injuries, he continued to lead the battle. This is one of the most tested biographical facts about Uhud.

mcqs.q: How many archers did the Prophet (PBUH) station on the mountain at Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: The Prophet (PBUH) stationed 50 archers on a strategic mountain pass (Jabal al-Ruma) at Uhud and strictly commanded them not to leave their posts. When the archers abandoned their posts assuming victory, the Quraysh cavalry circled around and attacked the Muslims from behind — turning the tide of battle.

mcqs.q: Who commanded the Quraysh forces in the Battle of Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: Abu Sufyan ibn Harb commanded the Quraysh forces at the Battle of Uhud. He was motivated by revenge for the Quraysh defeat at Badr and the death of his father-in-law Abu Jahl.

mcqs.q: Why was the Battle of Uhud fought?
options: As revenge for the Battle of Badr
To free Quraysh prisoners from Badr
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Uhud was fought as revenge for the Quraysh defeat at the Battle of Badr (2 A.H.). After their humiliating loss at Badr, the Quraysh organized a larger force of 3,000 soldiers led by Abu Sufyan and marched toward Madinah.

mcqs.q: Who separated from the Muslim army before the Battle of Uhud?
mcqs.explanation: Abdullah ibn Ubay ibn Salool, the leader of the hypocrites of Madinah, separated from the Muslim army before the Battle of Uhud along with 300 followers — reducing the Muslim force from 1,000 to 700.

mcqs.q: What is the other name of the Battle of Khandaq?
options: Battle of Ahzab / Battle of Trench
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Khandaq is also known as the Battle of Ahzab (Battle of the Confederates) or Battle of Trench. 'Ahzab' refers to the coalition of tribes that joined forces against the Muslims. 'Khandaq' means trench in Arabic.

mcqs.q: When did the Battle of Khandaq take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Khandaq took place in the month of Shawwal, 5 A.H. (627 AD). The opposing coalition numbered 10,000 fighters — the largest force assembled against Madinah at that time.

mcqs.q: Who suggested the strategy of digging a trench in the Battle of Khandaq?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Salman al-Farsi (RA) suggested digging a trench (khandaq) as a defensive strategy. He had seen this military tactic used in Persia. It was a completely novel idea in Arabia and proved highly effective against the enemy cavalry.

mcqs.q: How many days were spent digging the trench in the Battle of Khandaq?
mcqs.explanation: 6 days were spent digging the trench in the Battle of Khandaq. The Companions worked tirelessly under difficult conditions. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) himself participated in the digging.

mcqs.q: How many prayers did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) miss during the Battle of Khandaq?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) missed 4 prayers during the Battle of Khandaq due to the intensity of the siege. These were later made up (Qaza). This is a frequently tested fact about the Battle of Trench.

mcqs.q: How many enemy forces besieged Madinah in the Battle of Khandaq?
mcqs.explanation: 10,000 enemy forces (the confederated tribes) besieged Madinah in the Battle of Khandaq. The Muslims had only 3,000 defenders. The disparity of roughly 1:3 made the trench strategy essential to survival.

mcqs.q: Which was the 5th important battle of Islam?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Khaybar is described in sources as the 5th important battle of Islam. It took place in Muharram, 7 A.H. (628 AD). Khaybar was a Jewish fortification north of Madinah, and its conquest significantly strengthened the early Muslim state.

mcqs.q: When did the Battle of Khaybar take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Khaybar took place in the month of Muharram, 7 A.H. (628 AD). The Muslim force of 1,600 faced defenders of approximately 14,000 in their forts. The battle ended in a Muslim victory.

mcqs.q: What was prohibited by the Holy Prophet (PBUH) during Ghazwa Khaybar?
mcqs.explanation: The meat of the domestic donkey was prohibited by the Holy Prophet (PBUH) during Ghazwa Khaybar. This hadith is recorded in Sahih Bukhari and Muslim. The meat of wild donkey, however, remains permissible.

mcqs.q: When did the Battle of Mu'tah take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Mu'tah took place in the month of Jumada al-Awwal, 8 A.H. (629 AD). It was fought in the region of Mu'tah (in present-day Jordan) against the Byzantine Empire and its allied Arab tribes.

mcqs.q: Who was appointed as the first commander of the Muslim army at the Battle of Mu'tah?
mcqs.explanation: Zaid bin Haritha (RA), the freed slave and adopted son of the Holy Prophet (PBUH), was appointed as the first commander of the 3,000-strong Muslim army at Mu'tah. When he was martyred, command passed to Jafar ibn Abi Talib (RA), then to Abdullah ibn Rawaha (RA), and finally to Khalid bin Waleed (RA).

mcqs.q: How large was the Byzantine (Roman) army at the Battle of Mu'tah?
mcqs.explanation: The Byzantine army at the Battle of Mu'tah numbered 100,000 soldiers, with allied Arab tribes comprising an additional 100,000 — a total of approximately 200,000 opposing the 3,000 Muslims. Despite this impossible odds, Khalid bin Waleed (RA) executed a brilliant tactical withdrawal.

mcqs.q: What title was given to Khalid bin Waleed (RA) after the Battle of Mu'tah?
mcqs.explanation: After the Battle of Mu'tah, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) gave Khalid bin al-Walid (RA) the title Saifullah — Sword of Allah. This title recognised his extraordinary military skill in saving the Muslim army from total destruction against a vastly superior Byzantine force.

mcqs.q: In which year did the Truce of Hudaybiyyah take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Truce of Hudaybiyyah (Sulah Hudaybiyyah) took place in 6 A.H. (628 AD). It was a 10-year peace treaty between the Muslim state of Madinah and the Quraysh of Makkah. It paved the way for the Conquest of Makkah two years later.

mcqs.q: Who wrote the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) wrote the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah as the scribe of the Holy Prophet (PBUH). When the Quraysh objected to the phrase 'Muhammad, Messenger of Allah,' the Prophet asked Ali to remove it, but Ali (RA) was reluctant to strike out those words.

mcqs.q: By what name is the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah referred to in the Quran?
mcqs.explanation: The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah is referred to as Fatah al-Mubeen (Clear/Manifest Victory) in the Quran (Surah Al-Fath 48:1). Though it appeared as a concession at the time, it was a strategic triumph that led to the Conquest of Makkah.

mcqs.q: When did the Conquest of Makkah take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Conquest of Makkah (Fatah Makkah) took place in the month of Ramadan, 8 A.H. (630 AD). It is one of the most significant events in Islamic history, fulfilling the promise of a decisive victory.

mcqs.q: What is another name for the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: The Conquest of Makkah is also known as Aam-ul-Fatah (Year of Victory). It is also referred to as Fatah Makkah. 10,000 Muslim forces entered Makkah peacefully in this historic event.

mcqs.q: How many Muslims participated in the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: 10,000 Muslims participated in the Conquest of Makkah in 8 A.H. This was the largest Muslim force assembled up to that point. The Quraysh, overwhelmed, offered virtually no resistance.

mcqs.q: What was the colour of the Holy Prophet's flag at the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet's (PBUH) flag at the Conquest of Makkah was white and yellow. The flag colours of different Muslim commanders varied, but the Prophet's own standard at this conquest was white and yellow.

mcqs.q: How many days did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) stay in Makkah after its conquest?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) stayed in Makkah for 15 days after the Conquest. During this time he established order, assigned a governor, and ensured the transition to Islamic governance went smoothly.

mcqs.q: What is 'Tulaqa'?
options: Makkan pagans who accepted Islam after the conquest
mcqs.explanation: Tulaqa' refers to the Makkan pagans who accepted Islam after the Muslims conquered Makkah. The Prophet (PBUH) granted them a general amnesty, saying 'You are free.' Their voluntary submission strengthened the new Islamic state significantly.

mcqs.q: Who embraced Islam on the eve of the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: Ameer Muawiya (Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan) embraced Islam on the eve of the Conquest of Makkah. He later became a scribe of the Holy Prophet (PBUH) and went on to become the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate.

mcqs.q: Which Surah did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) recite at the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) recited Surah Al-Fath at the Conquest of Makkah. He also addressed the people at Koh-e-Safa (Mount Safa) after entering the city.

mcqs.q: Who was appointed as the first Wali (governor) of Makkah after its conquest?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Auttab bin Usaid (RA) was appointed as the first Wali (governor) of Makkah by the Holy Prophet (PBUH) after the conquest. He was a young Companion trusted with this important administrative responsibility.

mcqs.q: Where did the Holy Prophet (PBUH) address the people after the Conquest of Makkah?
mcqs.explanation: The Holy Prophet (PBUH) delivered his address at Koh-e-Safa (Mount Safa) after the Conquest of Makkah. From this elevated position he spoke to the gathered Makkans and extended the general amnesty.

mcqs.q: When did the Battle of Hunayn take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Hunayn took place in the month of Shawwal, 8 A.H. (630 AD) — very shortly after the Conquest of Makkah in the same year. It is mentioned in the Quran in Surah At-Tawbah (9:25–26).

mcqs.q: How many Muslims participated in the Battle of Hunayn?
mcqs.explanation: 12,000 Muslims participated in the Battle of Hunayn — the largest Muslim army at that time. Despite their numerical strength, the battle initially went against the Muslims due to an ambush, but eventually ended in a Muslim victory.

mcqs.q: Which was the last battle led personally by the Holy Prophet Muhammad (PBUH)?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Hunayn (Shawwal, 8 A.H., 630 AD) was the last battle led personally by the Holy Prophet (PBUH). The Battle of Tabuk (9 A.H.) is considered the last major expedition, but it did not result in actual combat.

mcqs.q: In which year did the Battle of Tabuk take place?
mcqs.explanation: The Battle of Tabuk took place in 9 A.H. (631 AD). It was the last major military expedition led by the Holy Prophet (PBUH). The Byzantine forces withdrew without fighting, making it a bloodless campaign.

mcqs.q: Who was the flag-bearer in Ghazwa Al-Abwa' (the first invasion)?
mcqs.explanation: Hazrat Hamzah ibn Abdul-Muttalib (RA) — the uncle of the Holy Prophet — was the flag-bearer in Ghazwa Al-Abwa' (Waddan), which took place in Safar 2 A.H. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) himself led this expedition.

mcqs.q: The Saif Al-Bahr Platoon was sent in which month and year?
mcqs.explanation: The Saif Al-Bahr Platoon was sent in Ramadan, 1 A.H. (623 AD). It is considered one of the earliest patrols dispatched in early Islamic history, under the command of Hazrat Ubayda ibn al-Harith (RA).

mcqs.q: In the Battle of Mu'tah, who was the commander of the Muslim army after three earlier commanders were martyred?
mcqs.explanation: After Zaid bin Haritha (RA), Jafar ibn Abi Talib (RA), and Abdullah ibn Rawaha (RA) were all martyred in sequence, Khalid bin Waleed (RA) took command. He executed a brilliant tactical withdrawal that saved the army, earning him the title Sword of Allah from the Prophet.

mcqs.q: What was the Muslim force at the Battle of Yarmouk?
mcqs.explanation: The Muslim force at the Battle of Yarmouk was 40,000, facing a Byzantine army of 240,000 — a ratio of 1:6. The battle was commanded by Khalid bin Waleed (RA) and is considered one of the most decisive battles in world history, securing Muslim control of the Levant (Syria/Palestine).

mcqs.q: Who commanded both the Battle of Walaj and the Battle of Chains?
mcqs.explanation: Khalid bin Waleed (RA) commanded both the Battle of Walaj (15,000 vs 30,000) and the Battle of Chains (18,000 vs 20,000). These were battles fought during the early Islamic conquests of Persia and Iraq under the caliphate of Hazrat Abu Bakr (RA).

mcqs.q: Which Companions accepted Islam after the Conquest of Makkah?
options: Abu Jahl and Amr ibn Hisham
Hind, Ikrima bin Abu Jahal and Sohail bin Amr
Abu Sufyan and Zaid bin Harith
mcqs.explanation: Among those who accepted Islam after the Conquest of Makkah were: Hind (wife of Abu Sufyan), Ikrima bin Abu Jahal, and Sohail bin Amr. All received the Prophet's general amnesty and became Muslims.

mcqs.q: The Truce of Hudaybiyyah was a treaty between Madinah and which party?
mcqs.explanation: The Truce of Hudaybiyyah was a treaty between Madinah (represented by the Holy Prophet PBUH) and the Quraysh tribe of Makkah. It was signed in 6 A.H. (March 628 AD) at the location of Hudaybiyyah on the outskirts of Makkah.
